evidence: The decl asks 'Is there a perfect Euler brick?' via IsPerfectCuboid, i.e. an Euler brick with an integer space diagonal.
File header 'Open questions regarding the existence of Euler bricks' references the Wikipedia Euler brick article; a perfect cuboid is an Euler brick with integer space diagonal.
Docstring 'Is there an Euler brick in 4-dimensional space?' generalizes the Euler brick via IsEulerHyperBrick.
Docstring 'Is there an Euler brick in n-dimensional space for any n > 3?' generalizes the Euler brick via IsEulerHyperBrick.
Docstring cites [Sh12]: a perfect Euler brick does not exist if all three Cuboid conjectures hold.
Docstring states Euler's sum of powers conjecture verbatim (sum of n k-th powers equal to a k-th power implies n >= k), open for k >= 6; file references the Wikipedia article of the same name.
Docstring: 'Euler's sum of powers conjecture is false for k=4 (counterexample exists)', proved with the 95800^4+217519^4+414560^4 counterexample.
Docstring: 'Euler's sum of powers conjecture is false for k=5 (counterexample exists)', proved with the Lander-Parkin counterexample 27^5+84^5+110^5+133^5=144^5.
Docstring '**Four exponentials conjecture**: ... some e^{x_i y_j} is transcendental' with the file referencing the Wikipedia Four exponentials conjecture article.
The conjecture asserts some exponential e^{x_i y_j} is transcendental (Transcendental Q in the statement).
Docstring: 'The four exponential conjecture would imply that for any irrational number t, at least one of 2^t and 3^t is transcendental.'
The statement asserts Transcendental Q (2^t) or Transcendental Q (3^t) for irrational t.
Docstring 'There are no distinct primes p and q such that (q^p-1)/(q-1) divides (p^q-1)/(p-1)' matches the Feit-Thompson conjecture; file references its Wikipedia article.
Docstring 'Are Fermat numbers composite for all n > 4?' in a file referencing the Wikipedia Fermat number article.
Compositeness of all Fermat numbers beyond n=4 is equivalent to there being no further Fermat primes.
Docstring 'Are there infinitely many Fermat primes?' is directly about Fermat primes.
Statement quantifies primality over n.fermatNumber, the Fermat numbers.
Docstring 'Are there infinitely many composite Fermat numbers?'
Docstring 'Are all Fermat numbers are square-free?' quantifies Squarefree over n.fermatNumber.
Docstring 'The **Fermat–Catalan conjecture** states that the equation a^m + b^n = c^k has only finitely many solutions...' with the file referencing the Wikipedia Fermat-Catalan conjecture article.
Docstring states the Darmon-Granville theorem, the fixed-exponent finiteness result toward the Fermat-Catalan conjecture.
Docstring 'There are infinitely many Fibonacci primes, i.e., Fibonacci numbers that are prime'; file references the Wikipedia Fibonacci prime article.
Docstring 'There are infinitely many indices i, such that the i-th Fibonacci is prime' — the index form of the Fibonacci prime conjecture.
Docstring '**Firoozbakht's conjecture**: The inequality (p_{n+1})^{1/(n+1)} < (p_n)^{1/n} holds for all prime numbers p_n'; file references the Wikipedia Firoozbakht's conjecture article.
Docstring: the prime-gap bound p_{n+1}-p_n < (log p_n)^2 - log p_n is 'A consequence of Firuzbakht's conjecture', taken as hypothesis P.
The statement bounds the prime gap (n+1).nth Prime - n.nth Prime by (log p_n)^2 - log p_n.
Docstring 'The Flint Hills series summing csc(n)^2 / n^3 from n=1 to infinity converges'; file references the MathWorld Flint Hills Series page.
